statement.markdown: Given a sorted array, remove the duplicates in place such that each element appears only once and return the new length. Do not allocate extra space for another array, you must do this by modifying the input array in-place with O(1) extra memory. The order of elements should be preserved.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, denoting the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n space-separated integers, denoting the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The first line contains an integer, denoting the new length of the array after removing duplicates.
statement.notes: You should modify the input array in-place.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The array after removing duplicates is [1, 2, 3], so the new length is 3.
The array after removing duplicates is [1, 2, 3], so the new length is 3.
The array after removing duplicates is [1], so the new length is 1.
The array after removing duplicates is [1], so the new length is 1.

hints.text: You can solve this problem by iterating over the array and comparing each element with its next one. If they are different, you can move on to the next pair. If they are the same, you can skip the current element.

Since the array is sorted, you can take advantage of this property to optimize your solution. You can use a two-pointer approach, one starting from the beginning of the array and the other from the second element.

The key insight here is that if the first pointer points to a unique element and the second pointer points to a duplicate, you can safely move the second pointer to the next element without affecting the uniqueness of the element at the first pointer.
The final step is to return the index of the last unique element, which is the new length of the array. This can be done by moving the first pointer to the end of the array and returning its index.

editorial.title: Removing Duplicates from Sorted Arrays Efficiently
editorial.intuition: The brute force approach to remove duplicates from a sorted array involves comparing each element with every other element, resulting in a high time complexity. However, the fact that the array is sorted provides a crucial insight: duplicates will be adjacent to each other. This observation unlocks a more efficient approach.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force method, although correct, is slow due to its comparison of each element with every other, leading to a time complexity of O(n^2). This makes it impractical for large arrays.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach would involve iterating through the array only once, leveraging its sorted nature to identify and remove duplicates in a single pass.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution, as provided, iterates through the sorted array with two pointers, one (i) keeping track of the position where the next non-duplicate element should be placed, and the other (j) scanning through the array. When a non-duplicate element is found (nums[j] != nums[i]), it is placed at the next position (i+1), effectively removing duplicates in-place. This approach achieves a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(1), making it highly efficient.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction. The base case is when the array has one or zero elements, in which case it is already sorted and has no duplicates. The inductive step involves showing that if the solution works for an array of size n, it also works for an array of size n+1. This is evident since the algorithm only moves forward, never revisiting elements, and correctly identifies and places non-duplicate elements in their correct positions.
editorial.dryRun: To validate the solution, one can perform a dry run with a sample array, such as [1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 5, 5], and verify that the output is indeed the length of the array without duplicates, which is 5 in this case.
commonMistakes: Not leveraging the sorted nature of the array
Using a brute force approach that compares each element with every other
Not correctly updating the pointers (i and j) during the iteration
Incorrectly assuming the need for additional space to store unique elements
Failing to handle edge cases, such as an empty array or an array with a single element
